title: To require the Secretary of State to report an assessment of the Conflict and Stabilization Operations Bureau, including whether the Bureau should be maintained, explanations of its function, and cost analysis of the Bureau.
plain_summary: This bill tells the Secretary of State to write a report about a government office called the Conflict and Stabilization Operations Bureau. The report must be finished within six months and explain what this office does and how much money it costs to run. The Secretary of State also needs to decide if this office should keep working or if it should be closed down. If they think it should be closed, they have to explain what would happen if that occurred.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1  Report on Function of Conflict and Stabilization Operations Bureau
sections.text: The Secretary of State must send a report to the right congressional committees within one hundred eighty days after this bill becomes law. The report must be about the Conflict and Stabilization Operations Bureau (called the Bureau in this bill). The report must include four main parts. First, it must give a clear answer about whether the Bureau should continue to exist. Second, it must explain what special job the Bureau does that no other office does. Third, it must look at how much money the Bureau costs to run. This cost analysis must include how much money it would cost to shut down the Bureau and how much money the government could save right away and over time if the Bureau was closed. Fourth, the report must include a plan that shows which jobs or workers from the Bureau might be moved to other offices in the Department of State if the Bureau is closed. The right congressional committees means the House of Representatives Committee on Foreign Affairs and the Senate Foreign Relations Committee.
